title: Dog Slow to Get Up From Bed: Causes, Urgency, and Care Tips
meta_desc: Is your dog struggling to stand up? Learn why dogs get stiff, when it's an emergency, and how to help your pet find relief from joint discomfort.
content_html:
What is causing my dog to be slow to rise?

When a dog is slow to get up from their bed, it is often a sign of stiffness or discomfort in the joints or spine. This is frequently seen in senior dogs and is commonly associated with conditions like osteoarthritis, hip dysplasia, or general age-related wear and tear. While it may seem like a natural part of aging, it usually indicates that your dog is experiencing some level of chronic pain that could be managed with veterinary help.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for a dog being slow to rise is generally Low. If your dog is still eating, drinking, and able to walk once they are up, you can typically wait for a scheduled appointment with your primary veterinarian. However, if the slow rising is accompanied by a complete inability to stand, yelping in pain, or dragging of the hind limbs, you should seek immediate emergency veterinary care.

How a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Taking a video of your dog attempting to stand up from their bed is one of the most helpful tools you can provide to a veterinary professional. Dogs often experience an adrenaline rush at the vet clinic, which can temporarily mask their pain and make them appear more mobile than they are at home. A clear video or photo of their resting position and their struggle to rise allows the vet to see their true symptoms in a relaxed environment, leading to a more accurate diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is it just old age or is my dog in pain?
faqs.answer: Age is not a disease, but it does come with conditions like arthritis. If your dog is slow to rise, they are likely experiencing some level of discomfort that can be treated.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human aspirin for stiffness?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human pain medications to dogs without a veterinarian's direct instruction, as many are toxic and can cause stomach ulcers or kidney failure.

faqs.question: What can I do at home to help them get up easier?
faqs.answer: Providing an orthopedic memory foam bed and using non-slip rugs on hardwood floors can provide better traction and support for dogs with mobility issues.
